coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective neurotransmitter epinephrine detection at a carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Elugoke, Saheed Eluwale
Fayemi, Omolola Esther
Adekunle, Abolanle Saheed
Ganesh, Pattan-Siddappa
Kim, Sang-Youn
Ebenso, Eno E.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the electrochemical detection of epinephrine (EP) at a carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was investigated. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs), copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs), and their composite (CQDs/CuO) were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–visible spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). A limit of detection (LoD) of 15.99 µM over a linear dynamic range (LDR) of 10–100 µM was obtained at GCE/CQDs/CuO using the square wave voltammetry (SWV) for EP electroanalysis at pH 7. The theoretical evidence for the conventional weak epinephrinequinone (EPQ) reduction peak and EPQ cyclization was investigated using the density functional theory (DFT) calculations. This sensor was also applied for the analysis of EP in chicken blood serum and EP injection with percentage recovery of 101.23 and 93.54 %, respectively. In addition to its outstanding long-term stability, good reproducibility, and ease of fabrication, the detection of EP in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA) was achieved at this sensor. The proposed sensor also demonstrated the ability to simultaneously detect epinephrine (EP), dopamine (DA), and uric acid (UA).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the electrochemical detection of epinephrine (EP) at a carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was investigated. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs), copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs), and their composite (CQDs/CuO) were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–visible spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). A limit of detection (LoD) of 15.99 µM over a linear dynamic range (LDR) of 10–100 µM was obtained at GCE/CQDs/CuO using the square wave voltammetry (SWV) for EP electroanalysis at pH 7. The theoretical evidence for the conventional weak epinephrinequinone (EPQ) reduction peak and EPQ cyclization was investigated using the density functional theory (DFT) calculations. This sensor was also applied for the analysis of EP in chicken blood serum and EP injection with percentage recovery of 101.23 and 93.54 %, respectively. In addition to its outstanding long-term stability, good reproducibility, and ease of fabrication, the detection of EP in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA) was achieved at this sensor. The proposed sensor also demonstrated the ability to simultaneously detect epinephrine (EP), dopamine (DA), and uric acid (UA).

ce:def-description.ce:para: Becke three exchange parameter, Lee, Yang and Parr hybrid functional of the 6–31 + G basis set

ce:section.ce:para: Epinephrine (EP) is a catecholamine neurotransmitter (NT) known for its active contribution to human mental alertness, especially under stress. EP is secreted by the adrenal medulla and plays a vital role in the hormonal and cardiovascular systems [1]. Like other catecholamine NT, epinephrine has been reported to be present in the human body at a very low concentration (about 11–14 nM) [2]. Abnormal EP level in the human system has been reported as a possible cause of Parkinson’s disease, abnormal heart rate, epilepsy, and anxiety disorder [3–5]. Analytical techniques such as fluorimetry, chromatography, and capillary electrophoresis have been adopted for the detection of EP with reasonable detection limits [6–8]. However, a simple, cheap, easily miniaturized method without elaborate sample preparation such as the electrochemical technique seems more promising for designing a point-of-care tool for EP and other NTs’ detection. Fortuitously, these unique attributes of the electrochemical technique have been demonstrated through the detection of various analytes in diverse matrices with encouraging outcomes such as high sensitivity, outstanding selectivity, good signal stability and reproducibility [9–12].

Over the last two decades, carbon nanomaterials have enjoyed great patronage in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors. Notably, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [13], conductive nanodiamonds [14], graphene [9,15], and carbon nanofibers [16] have been applied to modify bare electrodes to achieve sensing platforms that are not only sensitive to NTs but can equally detect NTs in the presence of interferents. These carbon nanomaterials possess good electrical conductivity, large surface area, and biocompatibility that result in improved sensitivity of electrodes modified solely with the carbon nanomaterial [17] or composites containing carbon nanomaterials [2,18,19]. These properties have been exploited for electrochemical EP detection [2,20,21]. For example, the high conductivity of multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) has been reported as the main reason behind the high sensitivity of MWCNTs/ZnO composite modified electrodes targeted toward EP detection [18]. Similarly, it has been reported that the modification of a bare electrode with graphene nanoribbon can improve the surface area and the electron transfer rate of the bare electrode for sensitive EP detection [22].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional members of the carbon nanomaterials family known for their large surface area, good conductivity and biocompatibility [23–25]. Unlike most carbon nanomaterials, CQDs often have average particle sizes of <10 nm [20,26,27]. This relatively small particle size, possession of similar electronic properties as CNTs, low toxicity, and the production of CQDs from very cheap precursors have made CQDs highly attractive nanomaterials for sensor fabrication. Like CNTs, carbon quantum dots often possess sp2 hybridized carbon atoms originating from graphitic carbon in the CQDs. Noteworthy, CQDs are carbonaceous materials containing predominantly sp2 and sp3 carbon masses [28]. CQDs have been combined with some other classes of materials for NTs detection with high sensitivity [29–31]. However, only a few instances where CQDs have been applied for electrochemical EP detection exist in literature [20,32]. A notable instance is the application of CQDs/molecularly imprinted polymers/graphitic carbon nitride composite for electrochemical EP detection [32].

Compared to the bulk counterpart, nano-sized metal oxides possess a larger surface area to volume ratio with attendant differences in physicochemical properties [33]. Metal oxide nanoparticles have a great reputation for high electrical conductivity (among other attributes) which makes them suitable catalysts for electrocatalytic oxidation of biomolecules. The fabrication of electrochemical sensors based on metal oxide nanoparticles has provided highly sensitive and selective EP sensors with good stability. Notably, titanium oxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), magnesium oxide (MgO), cerium oxide (CeO2), nickel oxide (NiO), ruthenium oxide (RuO2), and a host of other metal oxide nanomaterials have been successfully combined with other materials for the fabrication of highly sensitive EP sensors without the aid of an electron transfer mediator [34–38]. However, the actualization of an electrochemical sensing platform with high sensitivity, excellent selectivity in the presence of other biomolecules, great shelf-life, good stability, and low cytotoxicity without the assistance of an electron-transfer mediator is equally important. Copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) are biocompatible metal oxide nanoparticles with low toxicity. In addition, CuO NPs have greatly improved the electrocatalytic activity of bare electrodes toward NTs electro-oxidation both individually and as one of the components of composites [39–41]. CuO NPs enjoyed this level of patronage due to their biocompatibility, large surface area, and high electronic conductivity. Considering these attributes of CuO NPs, and the proven electrocatalytic activity of carbon-based quantum dots towards EP electro-oxidation, it is expected that the combination of CuO NPs and CQDs will offer an electrocatalytic platform for EP electro-oxidation with great biocompatibility, sensitivity, and very low toxicity. Furthermore, the cheap source of CQDs precursor and the ease of fabrication of the carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite modified electrode give the proposed sensor some advantages over some other carbon nanomaterial/metal oxide composite-based sensors [35,37].

This paper exploits the synergy between CQDs and CuO NPs in a CQDs/CuO nanocomposite for EP detection. The ability of the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to detect EP in a real sample and a mixture of dopamine, epinephrine (EP), and uric acid (AA) were also investigated. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first attempt at electrochemical EP detection at CQDs/CuO modified electrodes. Also, for the first time, a study on the theoretical basis for EP behaviour at CQDs/CuO modified electrodes with changes in scan rate was also done using the density functional theory (DFT) in conjunction with the UV–visible spectroscopy.

Epinephrine hydrochloride, epinephrine hydrochloride injection, copper sulphate pentahydrate (98 % CuSO4·5H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate (99 % Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (99.99 % NaH2PO4), hydrogen peroxide (35 % H2O2), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) (99 % K3Fe(CN)6), potassium permanganate (97 %), potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) (99 % K4Fe(CN)6), ethanol (99 %), dimethyl formamide (99.8 %), sodium hydroxide (99 % NaOH), ascorbic acid (99 %), uric acid (99 %), and dopamine hydrochloride (98 %), hydrofluoric acid (75 % HF) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich chemicals, South-Africa. HB pencils were obtained from a local market in South-Africa. The fresh chicken blood sample was collected from a local butchery.
The synthesis and characterization of CQDs, CuO nanoparticles, and the copper oxide/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) have been reported in our previous study [42].

Using the chemical precipitation method previously explored by Hwa et al. [42,43], copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) were synthesized from CuSO4·5H2O (0.2 M) and NaOH (0.1 M) precursors. About 80 mL of the NaOH solution was first heated at 80 °C and simultaneously stirred on a magnetic stirrer for thirty minutes. Afterwards, 50 mL of the CuSO4·5H2O solution was added dropwise with the aid of a burette. This mixture was heated and stirred for 10 min to obtain a black precipitate which was subsequently separated from the clear upper solution. This black precipitate was dried in a dry air oven for 12 h at 80 °C to obtain the crystalline CuO NPs.

According to Hummer’s method of quantum dots synthesis previously presented [42], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from a pencil graphite precursor. About 1 g of pencil graphite manually extracted (with a knife) from an HB pencil was transferred into a beaker already containing 6 g KMnO4 and 60 mL concentrated hydrofluoric acid. This mixture was agitated for about 10 min by sonication and subsequently refluxed for 1 h at 100 °C. The resultant mixture was cooled to room temperature before adding 10 mL H2O2. The suspension resulting from this was diluted and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The upper layer of fluorescent carbon quantum dots was separated from the lower less fluorescent quantum dots. The collected fluorescent CQDs particles were dried at 90 °C for six hours.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 4 mg CuO NPs and 4 mg CQDs was dissolved using 0.2 mL DMF. This mixture was ultrasonicated for 24 h at 25 °C to obtain a homogeneous paste. This paste (CQDs/CuO composite) was subsequently applied to modify GCE through the drop-casting technique [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots/copper oxide modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to modification, the bare GCE was thoroughly cleaned with alumina slurry on a cleaning pad. The GCE in this form was then sonicated in water and ethanol, respectively. Afterwards, the GCE was rinsed with distilled water and dried in the oven for 5 min at 50 °C. About 6 µL of the as-prepared CQDs/CuO nanocomposite was then dropped on the clean GCE and dried in the oven for 30 min at 50 °C [42]. This modified GCE (GCE/CQDs/CuO) was applied for epinephrine electroanalysis.

The epinephrine injection was diluted 1000 times by transferring 0.04 mL of the ampoule into 100 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7) prepared from a mixture of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. The resultant solution was diluted serially to obtain the desired 1000 times dilution [42]. The chicken blood sample was pre-treated using a method earlier adopted by Zhao et al. [44] The blood sample was transferred into a centrifuge tube and nitrated using 0.04 mL concentrated HNO3. The mixture was allowed to stand for 2 h and neutralized using 0.04 mL of 1 M NaOH solution. The resultant dispersion was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the upper clear serum solution. The serum was separated from the lower sediment and stored at 4 °C.

Characterization of the CQDs, CuO NPs, and their composite (CQDs/CuO) were done with the UV–visible, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The Spectroquantprove 600 spectrophotometer (Merck, Germany) was used for the UV–visible spectroscopy over 200–800 nm wavelength range. Opus Alpha-P FT-IR spectrophotometer (Brucker Corporation, USA) was used for the FT-IR spectroscopy data collection from 400 to 4000 cm−1 wave number. X-ray diffractograms were obtained with the aid of Rontgen X’Pert Pro diffractometer at a scan rate of 2° min−1. Scanning electron micrographs were obtained using a Quanta FEG 250 scanning electron microscope (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) after sample preparation (with ethanol) and deposition on mica substrate. Transmission electron micrograph for the particle size determination was obtained with the transmission electron microscope.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out at PGSAT-20 Metrohm Autolab potentiostat with the aid of Nova 2.1.3 software. The workstation is a three-electrode system with Ag/AgCl reference, Pt counter and GCE/CQD/CuO working electrode connected to the potentiostat. All electroanalysis were performed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7) unless otherwise stated. All square wave voltammetry (SWV) experiments were carried out at a frequency of 25 Hz, 5 mV potential step and modulation amplitude of 0.02 V. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were performed using an amplitude of 0.01 VRMS over a frequency range of 10−5–102 kHz. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) data were recorded at an amplitude of 0.025 V and a potential step of 0.005 V.

The theoretical studies were done using Gaussian 09 W software for the density functional theory (DFT) calculations. GaussView 5.0 was used for visualization. The Becke three exchange parameter, Lee, Yang and Parr hybrid functional of the 6–31 + G basis set was employed for the DFT calculation of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), the dipole moment (u), molecular electrostatic potential (MEP), and the electronegativity (X) of epinephrine and its oxidation and reduction products. Optimized structure of epinephrine (EP), epinephrinecrome (EPC), epinephrinequinone (EPQ), and leucoepinephrinechrome (LEC) were used for these calculations.

CuO NPs UV spectrum revealed absorptions at wavelengths of 290 and 355 nm, suggesting typical CuO NPs surface plasmon absorption [45]. CQDs UV spectrum in Fig. 1 A showed absorption at 290 nm due to n-π* transition of the inherent CO functionality. A similar UV–visible spectrum with peaks between 250 and 300 nm has been reported for CQDs prepared through electrochemical exfoliation of graphite [27]. In the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite, the peak at 339 nm in CuO NPs spectrum shifted to 348 nm, while the absorption at about 290 nm in the spectra of both nanomaterials (CQDs and CuO NPs) remained unchanged. The bathochromic shift in the spectrum of the nanocomposite has been reported to be indicative of strong interaction between two nanomaterials [46]. A similar redshift has been reported for Au nanoparticles after forming Au-graphene quantum dots composite (Au-GQDs) from Au nanoparticles and GQDs [46]. This change in absorption wavelengths in the composite indicates that a material that is different from the components has been formed.

The XRD diffractogram of CuO NPs (Fig. 1B) showed peaks at 32.7°, 35.5°, 38.7°, 48.8°, 53.7°, 59.3°, 61.6°, 66.2°, 68.0°, 72.5°, and 75.2° with corresponding miller indices of 110, 002, 111, 202, 020, 202, 113, 311, 113, 311 and 004, respectively. A similar diffraction pattern has been reported for the monoclinic phase of CuO NPs [47,48]. Using Debye-Scherrer’s equation, the CuO NPs crystallite particle size was calculated as 14.6 nm. The peaks appearing at 26.57° and 42.3° on the CQDs diffractogram (Fig. 1 B) suggest the presence of graphitic carbon. Specifically, the peak at 26.57° suggests the presence of coarse carbon materials [49]. Using Bragg’s equation (Equation (1)) where n = 1 and λ = 0.154 nm, the interplanar spacing calculated at 26.57° (θ = 26.57/2) was 0.34 nm. Interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm slightly defers from the value earlier reported for graphitic carbon (0.33 nm) [50]. The XRD diffractogram of the composite shows the disappearance of some peaks initially present on the CuO NPs spectrum (Fig. 1B). Specifically, the peaks at 32.7° (110), 35.5° (002), 38.7° (111), 48.8° (202), 53.7° (020), 59.5° (202), 61.6° (113), and 68.0° (113) originally found in the CuO NPs spectrum remained intact in the composite. Also, the peak found at 26.57° in the CQDs spectrum witnessed a drop in intensity in the nanocomposite. These outcomes prove that the composite was successfully made from individual nanomaterials.
(1)
2 d S i n θ = n λ

Fig. S1 shows the FT-IR spectra of CuO NPs, CQDs, and the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite. The spectrum of the CuO NPs shows absorption bands at 485 cm−1 and 688 cm−1 corresponding to Cu-O stretching vibration. This same spectrum showed another absorption band at 3382 cm−1, probably due to the –OH group in the moisture present in the CuO NPs [51,52]. A similar FT-IR spectrum for CuO NPs has been reported. The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs showed absorption at 1046, 3202, and 1639 cm−1 suggesting the presence of CO, –OH, and CC (from the graphitic precursor), respectively [53]. The –OH group is suspected to have emanated from the –COOH functionalities on the quantum dots. Noteworthy, the carboxylic group on the CQDs probably emerged after the oxidation of the graphitic precursor. This is evident in the absence of the peak at 3202 cm−1 in the spectrum of the pencil graphite precursor (Fig. 1C). The peak at 1046 cm−1 is probably due to the presence of CO of C-OH of the carboxylic/hydroxyl group [53,54]. The presence of C-F bonds in the CQDs could be inferred from the emergence of the peak at 718 cm−1. The nanocomposite FT-IR spectrum revealed absorption at 1101, 1742, and 3020 cm−1 due to the presence of CO, CO and –OH, respectively. The CO peak (of the carboxylic group) at 1742 cm−1 which was barely visible at 1755 cm−1 in the CQDs spectrum, became conspicuous after composite formation. The differences between the features of these peaks and the one on the CQDs spectrum is the intensity and a slight shift in wave number. For instance, the peak at 3020 cm−1 in the composite is weaker and broader than the peak at about 3020 cm−1 in the CQDs spectrum. Also, the absorption band at about 485 cm−1 in the nanocomposite is weaker than that of CQDs. In conclusion, the presence of most peaks initially found in the FT-IR spectra of CuO NPs and CQDs NPs in the spectrum of the composite is an indication that a composite comprising of these two materials has been formed.

The TGA curves of pencil graphite and CQDs in Fig. 1D show a distinct variation in the thermal stability of the pencil graphite precursor and the resultant CQDs in the presence of air. The TGA profile of pencil graphite shows the loss of adsorbed moisture from 0 −100 °C before about 11 % weight loss between 102 and 340 °C. The weight loss between 102 and 340 °C may be due to the decomposition of additives such as the binder (wax or resins) in the pencil graphite. The decomposition of graphite started at about 640 °C and ended at about 980 °C. The residual mass (about 68 %) could be the silicon-oxide or metal-based impurities in graphite. A similar TGA profile has been reported for pencil graphite [55]. Compared to pencil graphite, CQDs gave a residual mass of about 37 % and three different degradation stages. Like pencil graphite, there was water loss in CQDs up to 100 °C. The degradation between 102 and 330 °C, 374–640 °C, and 640–1110 °C may be due to the loss of carboxyl groups, other oxygen-containing functionalities, and the graphitic core in the CQDs, respectively [56–59]. The differences in the TGA profile of pencil graphite and CQDs confirm the transformation of the pencil graphite to CQDs.

The CQDs SEM micrograph (x 100, 000 magnification) shows that the CQDs particles are pseudo-spherical carbon flakes fused to form a large carbon mass (Fig. 2 A). At a lower magnification (x 5, 000), the CQDs appeared to be a fusion of carbon flakes with threadlike terminal parts [42]. The CuO NPs SEM micrograph (Fig. 2 B) revealed a random distribution of compact layers of pseudo-spherical particles. The nanocomposite SEM image (Fig. 2 C) revealed aggregates of the pseudo-spherical CuO NPs and CQDs (red circles) of different sizes. The difference in the sizes of the particles in the SEM image of the composite suggests that the composite is made of particles of different sizes. Also, the fact that the particles in the SEM image of the composite are largely pseudo-spherical implies that the individual nanomaterials retained their initial shape in the composite. The formation of this composite is suspected to be due to the possible van der Waal force of attraction between the components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of this TEM micrograph (Fig. 3 A) revealed that the CQDs have an average particle of 9.02 ± 0.18 nm. This was calculated from the average frequency density (AFD) of the diameter of the CQDs particles (Fig. 3B), which is synonymous with the average particle size. The average particle size calculated for these CQDs agrees with the average particle size of <10 nm previously reported for CQDs [53,60]. Noteworthy, the high percentage of copper and oxygen in the EDS spectrum of CuO NPs (Fig. 3C) supports every other evidence that CuO NPs have been successfully synthesized. Similarly, the EDS spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 3D) depicts high carbon content and a substantial amount of oxygen among other elements which exist in trace amounts. The presence of carbon and oxygen in this spectrum suggests the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups around the graphitic core of the CQDs while the trace elements are probably products of contamination during analysis.

The chemical composition and comprehensive structural analysis of the nanomaterials and composite were investigated with XPS. The full survey XPS spectrum of CQDs revealed strong peaks at 284 and 532 eV corresponding to C1s and O1s peaks (Fig. S 2A). The other peaks represent the presence of some other elements such as fluorine (F1s at 686.7 eV), which are either components of the starting material or contamination during analysis. The percentage composition of the CQDs from the CQDs XPS peak table confirmed that the CQDs is about 74.6 % carbon (Table S1). The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of the CQDs showed peaks at 284.2, 284.6 and 285.6 eV originating from CC (sp2), CC (sp3) and CO, respectively (Fig. 4 A). The O1s XPS spectrum of the CQDs revealed peaks at 529.8, 532, and 533.5 eV corresponding to the metal oxide, CO, and CO oxygen atoms, respectively (Fig. 4B). The functional groups identified with the XPS data agree with the FT-IR results which suggested the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the CQDs.

The XPS spectrum of CuO NPs revealed strong peaks at 529.7 and 936.9 eV originating from O1s and Cu2p, respectively (Fig. S 2B). The peak at 918 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p confirmed that the metal oxide nanoparticle is CuO NPs (Fig. 4 C). Cu 2p peaks of CuO NPs with similar binding energy have been reported [61]. The full scan spectrum of CQDs/CuO revealed strong peaks at 283, 530, and 937 eV originating from C1s, O1s, and Cu 2p, respectively (Fig. S 2C). The results confirmed the successful formation of the composite from CQDs and CuO NPs. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s and O1s in the composite are similar to that of CQDs (Fig. 4D and E), suggesting the presence of CQDs in the composite. Similarly, the high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p in the composite (Fig. 4 F) is similar to that of the composite, confirming the presence of CuO NPs in the composite.

The electrochemical characterization of all electrodes was carried out using 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7). Bare GCE, GCE/CQDs, GCE/CuO, and the nanocomposite modified GCE (GCE/CQDs/CuO) gave anodic peak current of 17.89 (at 0.27 V), 7.19 (at 0.28 V), 7.04 (at 0.14) and, 19.69 µA (at 0.37 V), respectively. The peaks recorded at higher potentials using GCE/CuO (at 0.56 V) and GCE/CQDs/CuO (at 0.72 V) are probably due to the oxidation of CuO NPs. The CuO NPs and the carbon dots have exhibited a synergy in the composite, which translates to better electrocatalytic activity towards the redox probe. The film thickness or electroactive surface area of the CQDs and the CuO NPs could have grossly affected their catalytic activity given the poor current response obtained at these electrodes relative to the bare GCE (Fig. 5 ). This phenomenon has been reported at a graphene oxide (GO) modified GCE where the GO modified GCE offered a lower current response in the presence of the redox probe [62]. The Randle Sevcik equation provides a means of computing the possible surface area of an electroactive material using Equation (2).
(2)

where A, D, v, C, n and ip represent the surface area of the electrode (cm2), the diffusion coefficient of the electroactive compound (cm2 s−1), scan rate (V s−1) concentration (mol cm−3), number of electrons (n) and peak current, respectively. The surface area calculated for bare GCE, GCE/CQDs, GCE/CuO, and the GCE/CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified GCE were 7.00, 2.81, 2.75, and 7.70 cm2, respectively. This indicates that the GCE/CQDs/CuO could be a much better electrode than the other three electrodes under study and was consequently applied for the electrocatalytic and electroanalysis study of epinephrine.

It is important to note that the electrochemical characterization of GCE/CQDs/CuO with a higher concentration of the redox probe (10 mM) gave a cyclic voltammogram with a slightly different shape [42]. This could be attributed to the high level of adsorption observed at the surface of the electrode at a higher concentration, going by the dark green colour of the surface of the modified electrode after the CV scan. With 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, the surface of the modified electrode gave a lighter colour, suggesting a concentration-dependent adsorption of the redox probe at the surface of GCE/CQDs/CuO which probably influenced the shape of the voltammogram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CuO and CQDs contents
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of the CQDs and CNPs in the composite were optimized by carrying out the electroanalysis of 0.4 mM EP (pH 7) at an electrode modified with CQD/CuO composites containing different fractions of CQDs and CuO NPs using cyclic voltammetry at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1. The three fractions of CQDs and CuO considered were ratios 1:1, 1:2, and 2:1 composed of 4 mg CQDs + 4 mg CuO NPs, 2 mg CQDs + 4 mg CuO NPs, and 4 mg CQDs + 2 mg CuO NPs, respectively. The electrodes modified with these composites were tagged GCE/CQDs/CuO (1:1), GCE/CQDs/CuO (1:2), and GCE/CQDs/CuO (1:1). The respective anodic peak currents (peak potential in parenthesis) recorded from the cyclic voltammograms of these electrodes were 26.90 (0.33), 25.18 (0.34), and 21.64 µA (0.37 V) (Fig. S 3A). The superior current response recorded at GCE/CQDs/CuO (1:1) informed the decision to use it for subsequent EP electroanalysis. This electrode was named GCE/CQDs/CuO and was so referred to throughout this study.

The effect of pH on the electrocatalytic oxidation of EP was investigated with 0.4 mM EP using CV at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer of pH ranging from 3.18 to 9.3 using CV. This pH range was selected because the multiple dissociation constants of phosphoric acid permits the preparation of pH buffer near pH levels of 2.2, 6.9 or 12.3 [63]. As seen in Fig. 6 A, the highest EP oxidation current response was obtained at a pH of 7.01 while the lowest was recorded at a pH of 5.13. The highly acidic nature of the medium (phosphate buffer) at pH 3.18 brought about the emergence of two prominent oxidation peaks. As earlier reported by Wierzbicka et al. [64], this peak at about 0.05 V (present about the same potential at all pH), which seems less pronounced at other pH values could be attributed to the oxidation of LEC. The relatively low pH at 3.18 supports the strong electrostatic attraction between the anionic group on the CQDs (on the modified electrode) and the protonated EP. The protonation at this pH could be as excessive as having the –OH of the EP aliphatic chain become a hydroxonium group. Since the peak beyond 0.2 V has been considered the EP oxidation peak in this study, the broader peak at 0.31 V was taken as the EP oxidation peak at pH 3.18. It is also noteworthy that the EPQ reduction peak is absent at pH 3.18. The higher oxidation peak at pH 7.01 could be due to the attraction between the cationic EP and the carboxylic group on the GCE/CQDs/CuO nanocomposite and π- π interaction between the duo. This is because it is much easier for EP to retain its cationic nature at a neutral pH without excessive protonation. This assertion was supported by the low current response recorded at pH 9.30. In this case, the deprotonation of the quaternary amino group probably led to repulsion between the modified electrode and the deprotonated EP, thus giving a very low EP oxidation current. The highest EP oxidation peak potential was obtained at a pH of 5.13, while the lowest was recorded at pH 3.18 (Fig. 6 B). The fact that a well-resolved EP oxidation peak, as well as EPC and EPQ reduction peaks, were recorded at a physiological pH of 7 explains why further studies were done at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GCE/CQDs/CuO electrocatalytic response to epinephrine oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic effect of the bare GCE and the modified electrodes was investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 in 0.4 mM EP prepared with 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7). The response of the bare GCE, carbon quantum dots modified GCE (GCE/CQDs), CuO NPs modified GCE (GCE/CuO), and nanocomposite modified GCE (GCE/CQDs/CuO) as seen in Fig. 7 A shows that the oxidation of EP occurred at all electrodes. Fig. 7 B depicts the pictorial representation of the current response at GCE/CQDs/CuO and other modified electrodes. The cyclic voltammograms of all electrodes showed four major peaks, namely the anodic current peak (A1) associated with the oxidation of EP (at about 0.2–0.33 V), the first cathodic peak (C1) at about 0.2–0.3 V and the second cathodic peak between −1 to −0.2 V (C2) (Scheme 1 A). Based on the electron transfer-chemical reaction-electron transfer (ECE) model presented by Sainz et al.[22] (Scheme 1B), C1 is due to the reduction of the epinephrine quinone (EPQ) to epinephrine chrome (EPC). At the same time, C2 emerged due to the reduction of epinephrine chrome (EPC) (Scheme 1B). Also, as seen in all voltammograms, epinephrine quinone (EPQ) transformation to EPC through Michael addition is highly promoted. The peak representing the oxidation of the very unstable leucoepinephrinechrome (A2) can be seen just before the EP oxidation peak in all voltammograms. Apart from the presence of A2 at GCE/CQDs/CuO, this peak (A2) was also evident at bare GCE (Fig. 7A). Noteworthy, no peaks could be found with bare GCE in pure phosphate buffer, suggesting that all the peaks found in EP (with bare GCE) came from the analyte (Fig. 7C). The emergence of well-defined peaks A1 and C1 in GCE/CQDs/CuO in the presence of EP (which were absent in pure phosphate buffer) confirm the catalytic effect of the composite. The effect of concentration and scan rate on this transformation will be discussed later.

The oxidation of EP and the Michael addition reactions were more catalysed at GCE/CQDs/CuO than at other electrodes. Precisely, the EP oxidation peak current at GCE/CQDs/CuO (26.99 µA at 0.32 V) is more than twice the one at GCE/CuO (12.22 µA at 0.20 V), almost thrice the value at the bare GCE (10.22 µA at 0.31 V) and about six times the anodic peak current at GCE/CQDs (4.91 µA at 0.30 V) (Fig. 7A). This could be ascribed to the large surface area and the synergistic effect of the CQDs and CuO nanomaterials in the composite. Specifically, the synergy between these materials stems from the large surface area and biocompatibility provided by the CQDs and the high conductivity of the CuO nanoparticles. Relatively, the transformation of EPQ to EPC is not as favourable at GCE/CuO as GCE/CQDs/CuO, but the reduction of EPQ was best achieved at this electrode (GCE/CuO). The poor redox peaks at GCE/CQDs suggest that the CQDs on the surface of the bare GCE (in GCE/CQDs) adversely affected the oxidation of EP and the transformation of EPQ to EPC. In fact, the peak corresponding to EPQ reduction is absent at GCE/CQDs. This indicates that the CQDs catalysed the deprotonation of the quaternary amino group between the EP aliphatic carbon atoms better than the bare GCE and the nanocomposite on GCE/CQDs/CuO such that EPQ is hardly formed.

The current responses at C2 were −29.6 µA at GCE/CQDs/CuO, −15.2 µA at GCE/CuO, −6.9 µA at bare GCE and −3.8 µA at GCE/CQDs. Using the ratio of A1 to C2, the reversibility of the redox process at the bare and modified electrodes is in the order GCE/CQDs/CuO (0.91) > GCE/CuO (0.80) > GCE/CQDs (1.29) > bare GCE (1.48). This trend implies that the synergy between the CQDs and CuO in GCE/CQDs/CuO provoked a more conducting electrocatalyst which catalysed EP oxidation and EPC reduction to a similar extent. This could be ascribed to a much larger surface area of the composite relative to the individual materials. Noteworthy, Michael addition requires both a donor and acceptor, which is the quinone form of EP (EPQ) in this instance. This 1, 4 addition process requires a base to deprotonate the cationic amino group of EPQ. In the present arrangement, the CQDs/CuO served as a base due to the possible carboxylate group on the CQDs, thus promoting the nucleophilic attack witnessed during the cyclization of EPQ. This possibility and the large surface area provided by introducing CuO NPs into CQDs improved the kinetics of this reaction. Evidently, the composite provoked the highest C2 relative to bare GCE, CQDs, and CuO.

The shift in the EP oxidation peak at GCE/CuO to the negative side relative to that of the bare GCE (Fig. 7A) is due to a drop in the overpotential of the EP oxidation at GCE/CuO, which fostered a better catalytic effect. Similar behaviour has been reported at poly(taurine) modified electrode for EP detection [65]. The EP oxidation peak at the CQDs/CuO modified GCE appeared close to that of the bare GCE and GCE/CQDs. Similar occurrences have been reported for the oxidation of catecholamine neurotransmitters at carbon-based quantum dots modified electrodes [27,66]. Specifically, the detection of dopamine at GCE/CQDs fabricated by Devi et al. [27] showed a slight shift of the oxidation peak to the positive direction relative to that of bare GCE. Similar behaviour can be seen in graphene quantum dots modified GCE synthesized by Vinoth et al. [66] for DA and EP detection. Interestingly, in these instances, the modified electrode offered better current responses. It is also worth noting that the square wave voltammogram of bare GCE and GCE/CQDs/CuO showed two peaks at – 0.20 and 0.32 V (Fig. S3B). The EP oxidation peak currents (at 0.32 V) recorded at both electrodes using SWV are lower than the values recorded with CV (Fig. S 3B). It is also worth noting that the SWV of the bare GCE and GCE/CQDs/CuO showed a significant difference at 0.32 V and a little difference at −0.2 V. Fortuitously, the peak at 0.32 V is the diagnostic EP oxidation peak.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data (Table S2) were collected using these electrodes in 0.4 mM EP solution prepared in phosphate buffer solution (pH 7). The error associated with each data was presented in parenthesis. The Nyquist plot of the EIS spectra of these electrodes, as seen in Fig. 8 A shows that the highest charge transfer resistance (Rct) was obtained at GCE/CQDs (450 kΩ) while the lowest was obtained at GCE/CQDs/CuO (6.72 kΩ). This implies that the nanocomposite gives the best conductive pathway for the transfer of electrons between the analyte and the modified electrode. On the other hand, the very high Rct obtained with GCE/CQDs suggests a very sluggish electron transfer by the CQDs. The CuO NPs modified GCE gave the closest Rct (17.22 kΩ) to that of GCE/CQDs/CuO and a higher Rct than the one obtained at bare GCE (24.28 kΩ) (Table S2). This shows the improvement in conductivity and increase in the rate of electron transfer at the bare GCE after modification with CuO NPs. Such improvement in conductivity of bare electrodes after CuO NPs modification has been reported [67]. Also, the diffusive process at the electrodes (except GCE/CQDs) can be inferred from the Warburg impedance (W) as seen in the Nyquist plot of the EIS spectra of the bare and modified electrodes (except GCE/CQDs). Conversely, the Nyquist plot of GCE/CQDs EIS spectrum did not show any visible Warburg impedance. The Randles equivalent circuit for GCE/CQDs has been represented with circuit X, while other electrodes were represented with circuit Y (Fig. 8 B). In comparison with other electrodes, the highest magnitude of the constant phase element (Yo) and the lowest value of N were found at GCE/CQDs/CuO. This value of N indicates that the GCE/CQDs/CuO possess the most significant departure from an ideal capacitance because the capacitive behaviour of a material improves as N gets closer to unity [68]. Yo is considered a pseudo-capacitance and mathematically has an inverse relationship with the impedance of the constant phase element (ZCPE) (since ZCPE = 1/(Yo(jω) n where j is an imaginary number and ω represents the angular frequency) [69]. The significantly high Yo obtained with GCE/CQDs/CuO compared to other electrodes indicates a very low value of ZCPE, confirming the superior conductivity of the composite modified electrode. Also, the lowest solution resistance (Rs) was obtained at the nanocomposite-modified GCE. These EIS data confirm the more conductive nature of GCE/CQDs/CuO (relative to other electrodes) and the results obtained from EP electroanalysis using CV (Fig. 7 A). Due to the superiority of GCE/CQDs/CuO as established with CV and EIS, GCE/CQDs/CuO was used as the working electrode for further electroanalysis of EP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate: Experimental and theoretical studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of scan rate on the electrocatalysis of EP was done with CV over a scan rate range of 25–400 mV s−1 in phosphate buffer (pH 7). As seen in Fig. 9 A, the oxidation of EP and the reduction of EPC increased with an increase in scan rate. Interestingly, the EPQ reduction peak did not change significantly with an increase in scan rate. The relationship of the square root of the scan rate with the EP oxidation peak (Iap) was more linear after 200 mV s−1 (higher scan rates), giving the relationship Iap = 3.7205 + 3.1073 v1/2 (R2 = 0.9918) while the EPC peak (Icp) increase with the square root of scan rate gave the linear relationship Icp = −20.8342–1.17305 v1/2 (R2 = −0.9837) (Fig. 9 B). The linear relationship between the square root of the scan rate and the two peaks suggests a diffusion-controlled process at the GCE/CQDs/CuO working electrode. A similar mechanism has been reported for the electrocatalysis of EP [22,70]. The CV in Fig. 9 A also shows a shift in the potential of the voltammograms as the scan rate changes. Precisely, the shift was more evident after 100 mV s−1. The relationship between the natural logarithm of the scan rate and the potentials (anodic and cathodic) were also linear (especially from 200 mV s−1). Noteworthy, the cathodic peak considered in this case is the reduction of EPQ (C2). The linear relationship between the anodic peak (Eap) and log v is Eap = 0.1481 + 0.1207 log v (R2 = 0.9982) while that of the cathodic peak against log v is Ecp = −0.1233–0.0639 log v (R2 = −0.9879) (Fig. 9C). The charge transfer coefficient of EP at the modified electrode (α) was calculated using the Laviron equation, where the slopes of Eap and Ecp against log v were given as b1 and b2, respectively. The slopes b1 and b2 were 0.1207 and 0.0639 V dec−1, respectively. The high value of the corresponding Tafel slope for the oxidation of EP (>120 mV dec−1) suggests a one-electron process at the EP oxidation rate determining step [1,64]. Eqs. (3) & (4) describe the slopes b1 and b2 respectively, where n, R, T, and F represent the number of electrons transferred, molar gas constant (J mol−1 K−1), absolute temperature (K) and, Faraday’s constant (C mol−1).

From Eqs. (3) and (4), n and α were calculated as 1.41 and 0.65, respectively. This value of n (n ∼ 1) is similar to the value reported for the number of electrons transferred in the rate-determining step of EP oxidation at the surface of a chemically modified electrode [64]. The value of α obtained here is higher than that obtained at a nanoporous thin gold film prepared by Wierzbicka et al. [64] (0.52) and the dysprosium tungstate modified screen-printed electrode presented by Beitollahi et al.[1] (0.30). This difference could be attributed to the difference in the conductivity and the surface area of the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite compared to the modifiers used in these sensors. The charge transfer rate constant (ks) was calculated using Equation (5). The value of ks obtained at this electrode is 0.025 s−1.

To understand the behaviour of EP and its redox products with a change in scan rate, the change in the absorbance of a fixed concentration of EP with an increase in scan rate was monitored at 280 nm (EP absorption maximum). According to Beer-Lambert’s law, the EP absorbance is expected to increase with an increase in concentration as seen in Fig. 9 D. Using a constant EP concentration (68 µM) at pH 7, the scan rate was raised at intervals from 25 mV s−1 to 400 mV s−1 to obtain the voltammograms in Fig. 9 E. Interestingly, the EP absorbance increased with increase in the scan rates except for a decline at 200 mV s−1 (Fig. 9 F). The significantly low absorbance at lower scan rates and the barely visible EPQ reduction peaks at these low scan rates suggest that (1) low concentration of EP limits the quantity of the other electroactive species and (2) the increase in absorbance could be due to the presence of LEC, EPQ and EPC in solution after initiating the oxidation process at 25 mV s−1.

To validate this suspicion, the chemical properties of the four forms of EP were obtained using the density functional theory (DFT) calculations obtained from the Gaussian 09 W software with the aid of the B3LYP/6–31 + G basis set. The second row of Table 1 shows the optimized structures of EP, EPQ, EPC, and LEC with their respective molecular electrostatic potential (MEP). The MEP map showed high electron density coverage (in blue) in all species to a similar extent. The third and the fourth row in the table show the Frontier molecular orbitals of the four species. Specifically, the third row showed the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO), while the fourth showed the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Table S3 shows the HOMO energy (EHOMO), LUMO energy (ELUMO), electronegativity (X), and dipole moment (u) of the electroactive species. These data revealed that the HOMO-LUMO energy gap (ΔE) for EP is higher than the other three species. Since the molar extinction coefficient has a direct link with the energy gap, the two other species would be expected to absorb at wavelengths quite different from that of EP. Also, LEC has the closest ΔE value to EP, and that probably explains the rise in absorbance after the first CV scan at 25 mV s−1 and the shape of the voltammogram (Fig. 9 E). The voltammogram shows the LEC oxidation peak and the EPC reduction peaks, but the EPQ reduction peak could barely be seen due to the possible spontaneous transformation of the available EPQ to EPC. This suggests that the rise in absorbance is due to the contribution from LEC and EPC which is favourably formed from EPQ cyclization due to the low kinetic stability of EPQ as inferred from its very low ΔE [71]. Noteworthy, a molecule with low ΔE tends to be very reactive [72]. In this case, the very little available EPQ rapidly undergoes cyclization thereby contributing very little (if at all) to the recorded absorbance.

The drop in absorbance at 200 mV s−1 could be down to two fundamental processes. One, the adsorption of a large quantity of EP and its oxidation products to GCE/CQDs/CuO which reduced the availability of EP availability and the other three species (at 200 mV s−1) compared to what obtains at 150 mV s−1. Two, the reactive nature of EPQ could also support its adsorption to the modified electrode, thus limiting the quantity of EPQ available for 1, 4 addition. The seemingly indistinguishable EPC reduction peaks support this second prediction at 200 mV s−1 and 250 mV s−1 which signals the possible drop in the quantity of EPC in solution.

This disruption in the progressive increase in absorbance with scan rate suggests that the EP redox process might not solely be a diffusion-controlled reaction. The possibility of a surface-confined process can also be inferred from the linear relationship between Iap and v from the curve in Fig. 9 E (graph not shown). Also, the CV of the modified electrode in pure phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7) after initially subjecting the modified electrode to 20 CV scans in 0.4 mM EP and subsequent rinsing with a copious amount of the buffer showed weak peaks at about 0.32 V (Fig. S 3C), suggesting slight adsorption of EP to the electrode. Apart from the highest value of absorbance, LEC oxidation, EPQ reduction, and EP oxidation currents recorded at 400 mV s−1, there was also a drop in the EPC reduction current relative to that obtained at 350 mV s−1. This confirms that the absorbance recorded at the absorption maximum for EP (280 nm) was contributed by the four species but in different capacities. Considering these outcomes, it can be concluded that:
i.
EP, EPQ, EPC and LEC are liberated in different proportions when the EP cyclic voltammetry is carried out at GCE/CQDs/CuO with varying scan rates.
ii.
The weak EPQ reduction peak in the CV of EP may be due to the low kinetic stability of EPQ (given its low ΔE value) which promotes its rapid cyclization, thus making its presence in solution quite transient.
iii.
The MEP map for EP and LEC look similar, possibly because the benzene ring in both species remained intact and contributed equally to the molecular orbital energies. This indicates a possible prevalence of π-π interaction between EP (or LEC) and the graphitic region of the CQDs.
iv.
EP electroanalysis at GCE/CQDs/CuO is both diffusion and surface-controlled.

The effect of concentration on the EP redox processes at the modified electrode was investigated using square wave voltammetry (SWV) by increasing EP concentration from 10 to 116.4 µM in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7). Before the actual concentration studies, the CQDs/CuO modified electrode was stabilized by running 20 consecutive CV scans at 25 mV s−1 in pure phosphate buffer (pH 7) (Fig. S 3D). The square wave voltammogram showed that peak current increased with an increase in EP concentration (Fig. 10 A). This increase gave a linear relationship between the peak current and EP concentration represented by a regression equation
I
a p
= 5.6826 + 0.0379
E P

R

= 0.99
78) (Fig. 10 B). From this linear relationship, the limit of detection of the modified electrode was calculated using the relationship 3.3 ε/m where ε and m represent the intercept’s standard deviation and the slope of the curve, respectively.

The LoD of this sensor was calculated as 15.99 µM. As seen in Fig. 10 B, this relationship was linear over a range of 10–100 µM. This LoD is comparable to that of some carbon nanomaterial-based sensors [73,74] (Table 2 ). Also, the linear range obtained at this electrode is wider than the dynamic range reported by Mphuthi et al. [74] at magnetic nanoparticles, phthalocyanine, and multi-walled CNTs composite modified GCE. These outcomes suggest that the proposed sensor is reliable for micromolar EP detection for diagnosis associated with EP concentration, especially those suspected to be connected to high EP concentration.

It is common knowledge that EP, just like every other monoamine neurotransmitter co-exists with some other biomolecules such as ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) in the extracellular fluid. These interfering molecules exist at much higher concentrations in the body fluid relative to epinephrine [75]. As such, we modelled this scenario by investigating the possible interference of 1 mM AA peak with 0.4 mM EP current signal. Using GCQ/CQDs/CuO and the bare GCE for CV, the AA and EP peaks were indistinguishable (Fig. S 4A). This is probably because the AA and EP peaks were detected at similar potentials. With differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), well-resolved AA peak (found at 0.24 V) and EP signal (found at 0.39 V) were recorded at GCE/CQDs/CuO. The better AA-EP peak separation (230 mV) obtained with SWV compared to the lower peak separation with DPV (150 mV) informed the choice of SWV for interference studies (Fig. S 4B & C). While the AA concentration remained constant (1 mM), the concentration of EP was varied between 62.4 and 140.4 µM. The voltammograms in Fig. S 4D showed a progressive increase in EP peak currents while there was no significant change in the AA peak. Although the presence of AA significantly reduced the EP current response when the square wave voltammogram in the presence and absence of AA are compared (Fig. S 3B & S 4D), the discriminative EP detection suggests that the sensor could be suitable for EP detection in the presence of other interfering molecules in real samples. The interference of AA with EP, which often occurs due to the closeness of the oxidation peaks of AA and DA has previously been reported [76]. In the present study, the interference of AA with DA signal could also be ascribed to the competitive interaction of the AA and DA with the CQDs in the composite via the dominant π-π interaction. Interference of AA with DA due to competitive binding of the analytes with a carbon nanomaterial-supported composite has been reported [77]. In addition, the slight interference of AA with DA suggests that intermolecular force of attraction such as hydrogen bonding between the electrode modifier and the analytes is dominant over the expected electrostatic interaction (or repulsion) between the analytes and the electrode modifier.

Moreover, the selective detection of EP in a mixture of dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), and EP was investigated through the electroanalysis of a mixture of 3 mL 0.4 mM EP, 3 mL 0.4 mM DA, and 3 mL 5 mM UA using square wave voltammetry (SWV). The resultant voltammogram shows peaks at 0.06, 0.28, and 0.41 V, corresponding to the DA, EP and UA peaks, respectively (Fig. S 4E). This outcome shows slight variation in the peak potentials of EP in the absence of DA and UA. It is worth noting that a shift in EP oxidation potential to the negative side in the presence of UA has been reported [78]. Similarly, the voltammogram (Fig. S 4E) showed that the DA peak in the presence of EP and UA significantly differed from the peak potential previously reported for DA alone (0.36) [42]. The significant change in DA peak potential could be attributed to the strong affinity of the modified electrode for UA which manifested in preferential binding of UA to the modified electrode. It is expected that the UA oxidation product would significantly influence the surface chemistry and the electrocatalytic effect of the CQDs/CuO composite on DA oxidation. Consequently, the DA oxidation peak potential significantly shifted to the negative side in the presence of UA relative to the DA potential in the absence of UA and EP. The affinity of the sensor for UA further confirms the possible dominance of intermolecular force of attraction (such as hydrogen bonding) between the analytes and the modified electrode. This affinity could be due to the presence of numerous free and hydrogen bonded heteroatoms in UA which supports hydrogen bonding with the CQDs in the composite modified electrode. Despite this outcome, the successful electroanalysis of EP in the presence of DA and UA as well as AA at this sensor indicates that EP can be selectively detected in the presence of other biomolecules at the proposed sensor.

The EP real sample analysis was done by analysing epinephrine in epinephrine hydrochloride injection and chicken blood serum using the standard addition technique as adopted by Shaikshavali et al.[18] Using the epinephrine hydrochloride injection and the blood serum, percentage recovery of 101.23 % and 93.54 %, respectively were recorded. The low percentage relative standard deviation (% RSD) of 7.56 % and 5.29 % obtained from the respective real samples, as shown in Table S4, is a strong indication that the fabricated sensor is suitable for the point-of-care analysis of epinephrine.

The reproducibility of the electrode was investigated by modifying the electrode three different times using the same concentration of the analyte under the same condition (pH 7, scan rate 25 mV s−1) and measuring the current response at each electrode with CV. The EP oxidation current obtained after the three EP electroanalysis trials gave a percentage relative standard deviation (% RSD) of 9.59 % (Fig. S 5A), suggesting that the electrode modification process can be replicated with little variation in output. Using the same approach, the cyclic voltammograms recorded in pure phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7) also appeared reproducible (Fig. S 5B).

The stability of the electrode after repeated CV scans was investigated by subjecting the modified electrode in the EP solution to 20 CV scans at 25 mV s−1 (Fig. S 5C). After the CV scans, the current dropped to about 83.71 % of the current recorded after the first scan. Based on the fact that some other sensors suffered worse current drops after CV scans [79], it can be concluded that the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified GCE is relatively stable and less susceptible to fouling.

The shelf-life (long-term stability) of the sensor was investigated through the electroanalysis of 0.4 mM EP (pH 7) using CV at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 over a period of thirteen days at three days intervals (Fig. S 5D). The cyclic voltammograms recorded revealed that the sensor retained 97.65 % of its initial anodic peak current (anodic peak current on day 1) at the end of the 13th day (Fig. S 5D). This residual current is higher than the value reported for the shelf-life of a chemically modified electrode (designed for EP electroanalysis) over a similar time frame [80]. It is also worth noting that the % RSD of the anodic peak currents recorded over 13 days period 2.85 %.

The detection of EP at CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified GCE was investigated for the first time in this study. The theoretical evidence for the transient nature of EPQ and its smaller reduction peak relative to other current peaks was also established in this study. The sensor offered a relatively low detection limit and wide dynamic linear range compared to some other earlier fabricated sensors. This sensor was also successful in discriminative EP detection in the presence of AA. Selective detection of EP in the presence of dopamine and uric acid was also achieved at this sensor. The excellent shelf-life and the repeatability of the modification process using the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite with little change in EP oxidation current are major features of this sensor. These attributes of GCE/CQDs/CuO among others suggest that the electrode can be repeatedly fabricated with reliable output. In addition, its outstanding repeatability implies that the large-scale commercialization of the sensor for point-of-care use can be confidently explored.
